did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning and deep learning methods for naevi classification in dermatology. Specifically, it employed Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Convolutional Autoencoders (CAE) for automated feature extraction from dermoscopy images, followed by classification using Random Forest (RF) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN). The ANN model achieved an accuracy of 95.62% and sensitivity of 100% in distinguishing suspicious from non-suspicious naevi.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving machine learning and deep learning models (PCA, CAE, RF, ANN) for classifying suspicious naevi. It details specific methods, datasets (HAM10000), experimental results (accuracy, sensitivity metrics), and new findings, published in the 2022 International Conference on Digital Image Computing: Techniques and Applications (DICTA).

title: Identification of Suspicious Naevi in Dermoscopy Images by Learning Their Appearance
journal: 2022 International Conference on Digital Image Computing: Techniques and Applications (DICTA)
brief_summary: This study aimed to identify suspicious naevi in dermoscopy images to aid in early melanoma detection by classifying them as suspicious or non-suspicious. The researchers utilized a dataset of 28,222 dermoscopic images from 59 participants, derived from the HAM10000 dataset, to train machine learning models. Automated feature extraction was performed using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Convolutional Autoencoders (CAE), followed by classification with Random Forest (RF) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) models. The ANN model combined with CAE features achieved the highest performance, with an accuracy of 95.62%, sensitivity of 100%, specificity of 91.24%, and AUC of 95.6%. The results suggest that machine learning approaches can effectively characterize naevus appearance and potentially serve as a guide for clinicians in identifying suspicious lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on classifying naevi into two categories: suspicious (requiring further follow-up) and non-suspicious. The dataset contained 26,606 images of non-suspicious naevi and 1,616 images of suspicious naevi derived from the HAM10000 dataset. The term 'ugly duckling naevus' is used to describe suspicious naevi that do not fit the dominant partitions of an individual's naevus.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification of dermoscopy images into two discrete diagnostic categories: suspicious naevi (requiring further follow-up) and non-suspicious naevi. The study employed Random Forest (RF) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifiers to predict these labels based on features extracted via PCA and Convolutional Autoencoders (CAE).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A subset of the HAM10000 dataset, a large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The specific subset used in this study was provided by The University of Queensland Diamantina Institute, Dermatology Research Centre. It consists of dermoscopic images from 59 study participants (aged 14-73 years) with lesions annotated by a certified melanographer. The dataset was categorized into suspicious and non-suspicious naevi based on HAM10000 labels and clinical follow-up requirements.

use.training: Used for training machine learning models (PCA, CAE, RF, ANN) to classify naevi. The dataset was split 75% for training and 25% for testing in some contexts, but the primary split for the final reported results is not explicitly defined as a fixed train/val/test split in the text, though resampling (SMOTE, cluster centroids) was applied to handle class imbalance.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's generalizability and evaluating performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, AUC).
use.other: The dataset was also used to extract 399 pre-processing features (asymmetry, border, colour, texture, pixel size, lesion area) and to generate eigen-naevi images via PCA.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Dimensionality reduction of the dataset to extract prominent appearance features and eigen-naevi images.
Classification of naevi and ranking of feature importance to select the most important features for distinguishing suspicious from non-suspicious naevi.
Classification of naevi into suspicious and non-suspicious categories based on extracted features.
Automated feature extraction from dermoscopy images.
Handling class imbalance in the dataset by oversampling the minority class (suspicious naevi) prior to model training.
Handling class imbalance in the dataset by under-sampling the majority class (non-suspicious naevi) prior to model training.
Weight optimization during the training of the Convolutional Autoencoder.
Loss function used to train the Convolutional Autoencoder.
Deep convolutional CNN implementation for classification on the same dataset to compare performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification metrics (accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, precision, AUC) and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

PCA_Features_RF.notes: RF ranked PC1, PC2, PC3, PC4, and PC9 as the most important features. PC9 was noted to capture the appearance of suspicious naevi.

PCA_Features_Ann.notes: Dataset split: 45 patients for training, 14 patients for testing. 20% of training data used as validation.

CAE_Features_RF.notes: 4096 features extracted from CAE encoder used as input.

CAE_Features_Ann.notes: The ANN with CAE features and under-sampling achieved the highest performance. The confusion matrix for this model showed 1 true positive for suspicious naevi and 0 false negatives (sensitivity 100%).

Resnet50.notes: Resnet50 was implemented on the same dataset for comparison.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Autoencoder (CAE) for feature extraction; Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and Random Forest (RF) for classification. ResNet50 used for comparison.

CAE.layers: 7 convolutional layers total (3 for encoder, 4 for decoder)

ANN.structure: Input layer, two dense hidden layers, output layer
ANN.activation_function: Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) for hidden layers

RF.description: Ensemble of decision trees using majority decision

ResNet50.description: Used for comparison on the same dataset

training_details.dataset_split: 45 patients for training, 14 patients for testing (20% of training data used as validation)

classification_or_output_head.ANN_output: Single logistic sigmoid neuron for binary classification (suspicious vs non-suspicious)
classification_or_output_head.RF_output: Classification based on ensemble of decision trees

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of input images into vectors
Normalization of vectors to ensure unique characteristics
Resizing/Conversion to the same image space to preserve pixel correspondence prior to PCA processing
Application of PCA for dimensionality reduction and feature extraction
Application of Convolutional Autoencoder (CAE) for automated feature extraction
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) for handling class imbalance
Cluster Centroids Under-sampling for handling class imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for oversampling the minority class (suspicious naevi) to handle class imbalance in the dataset prior to model training.
Used for under-sampling the majority class (non-suspicious naevi) to handle class imbalance in the dataset prior to model training.

conclusions: The Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model combined with features extracted by Convolutional Autoencoders (CAE) and under-sampling achieved the highest performance, with an accuracy of 95.62%, specificity of 91.24%, sensitivity of 100%, precision of 91.95%, and AUC of 95.6%.
Random Forest (RF) classification using features from both PCA and CAE achieved an overall accuracy of 88.46%.

RF feature ranking identified PC1, PC2, PC3, PC4, and PC9 as the most important features for distinguishing suspicious from non-suspicious naevi, with PC9 specifically capturing the appearance of suspicious naevi.
PCA was able to reduce the dataset dimensionality to 900 principal components, which represented approximately 98% of the main features.
Resampling methods, specifically cluster centroids under-sampling and SMOTE oversampling, were effective in handling class imbalance and obtaining more accurate outcomes.
The ResNet50 deep convolutional CNN achieved an accuracy of 88.96%, specificity of 90.97%, sensitivity of 86.96%, precision of 90.65%, and AUC of 94% on the same dataset.

Machine learning approaches can effectively characterize naevus appearance and may serve as an efficient guide for clinicians in the early detection of melanoma by identifying suspicious naevi, pending clinical validation.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small patient-level split (45 patients for training, 14 for testing) derived from the HAM10000 dataset, which may limit the generalizability of the results compared to standard image-level splits.

The Convolutional Autoencoder (CAE) model has a high number of trainable parameters and is noted to tend towards overfitting on this small dataset; the authors suggest reconstruction helps mitigate this by learning both appearance and structure.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance (26,606 non-suspicious vs. 1,616 suspicious naevi), requiring the use of resampling techniques (SMOTE and Cluster Centroids Under-sampling) to achieve high sensitivity.

The best-performing model (ANN with CAE features and under-sampling) achieved 100% sensitivity but had a precision of 91.95%, meaning 4.38% of naevi were misclassified (specifically, 69 suspicious naevi were classified as non-suspicious in the confusion matrix analysis described in the text, though the sensitivity metric implies 0 false negatives in the final reported model context, there is a discrepancy in the text's description of the confusion matrix vs the metric summary).
The paper acknowledges that clinical validation is pending before these machine learning approaches can serve as an efficient guide for clinicians in early melanoma detection.

The study defines 'suspicious' naevi based on HAM10000 labels and clinical follow-up requirements rather than a strict histopathological gold standard for all cases, which may introduce label noise or bias regarding the definition of suspiciousness.
